<!-- Topic 2: Memory Leaks -->
<!-- Total slides: 5 -->

# Memory Leaks

---

## What happens when nobody calls delete? {.smaller}

+ The heap block is reserved for the life of the program — but nothing can reach it anymore.
+ No crash, no error message. Just memory that is silently gone.

::: notes
Total slides: 5
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## A leak in slow motion

```{.cpp}
void loadData() {
    int* buffer = new int[1000];
    // ... process buffer ...
    // forgot to delete[] buffer
}   // buffer (the pointer) is destroyed here
    // the heap block it pointed to is not
```

- The pointer `buffer` was a local variable — it goes away when `loadData` returns
- The 1000-element heap block has no pointer left pointing to it
- There is now no way to call `delete[]` on it — it is leaked for the rest of the program

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Why leaks are dangerous

- A single call to `loadData()` wastes 4 KB — not a problem
- Called in a loop 100,000 times: 400 MB gone silently
- Long-running programs (servers, games) accumulate leaks until the OS kills them
- The program continues running — there is nothing to catch

::: notes
[Graphic suggestion: bar chart showing heap usage growing each loop iteration as the function is called repeatedly, while the program appears to function normally]
:::

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## The ownership question

Every heap allocation must have one clear owner — the piece of code responsible for calling `delete`.

- If the allocator and the deallocator are in the same function: straightforward
- If ownership transfers (e.g. a function returns a pointer): document it explicitly
- If two pieces of code both think they own it: double-delete, undefined behavior
- If neither does: leak

::: notes
The ownership problem is why smart pointers exist. unique_ptr encodes "exactly one owner"; shared_ptr encodes "shared ownership with reference counting." We will revisit those shortly.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Summary

+ A memory leak occurs when a heap allocation has no remaining pointer to reach it — it cannot be freed.
+ The fix is clear ownership: every `new` must have exactly one designated `delete`, in a place that is guaranteed to execute.

<!-- Slide 5 -->
